explicit_text: I grew up in San Jose, California, where my family worked in orchards and sharecropping. Before the war, we lived a hard-working life, waking up early to irrigate raspberries and cucumbers. After the internment, when we returned, we stayed at the San Jose Buddhist Church and even slept on the floor of the Methodist Church. It was tough coming back to San Jose because jobs were scarce and there was a lot of prejudice against Japanese Americans.
implicit_text: I grew up in a major city in California's Silicon Valley, where my family worked in orchards and sharecropping. Before the war, we lived a hard-working life, waking up early to irrigate raspberries and cucumbers. After the internment, when we returned, we stayed at the local Buddhist Church and even slept on the floor of the Methodist Church. It was tough coming back to this city because jobs were scarce and there was a lot of prejudice against Japanese Americans.

explicit_text: When the presidential proclamation came out, my family and I were sent from San Jose to the Santa Anita Assembly Center. We stayed there for about three months while a more permanent camp was being built. Santa Anita was tough; we lived in a parking lot or stables that smelled awful, where horses like Seabiscuit and Man o' War were kept. There was also a riot once, and we had to run for our lives back to our barracks. The Zanker family from San Jose helped us get to the railroad station when we left for Santa Anita.
implicit_text: When the presidential proclamation came out, my family and I were sent from the California city to a temporary assembly center located at a racetrack in Southern California. We stayed there for about three months while a more permanent camp was being built. That assembly center was tough; we lived in a parking lot or stables that smelled awful, where famous racehorses like Seabiscuit and Man o' War were kept. There was also a riot once, and we had to run for our lives back to our barracks. The Zanker family from my hometown helped us get to the railroad station when we left for that assembly center.

explicit_text: After Santa Anita, we were sent to Heart Mountain Relocation Center in Wyoming, a desolate place where about 10,000 Japanese Americans were confined. I was there for nearly three years, until I graduated high school. Life was hard and cold, with temperatures dropping to minus 28 degrees. The camp was tightly controlled with guards and searchlights, and we even got punished for sledding outside the fence. Despite that, I found some freedom in sports and worked in places like the tire shop and driving tractors.
implicit_text: After the assembly center, we were sent to a relocation center in Wyoming, a desolate place where about 10,000 Japanese Americans were confined. I was there for nearly three years, until I graduated high school. Life was hard and cold, with temperatures dropping to minus 28 degrees. The camp was tightly controlled with guards and searchlights, and we even got punished for sledding outside the fence. Despite that, I found some freedom in sports and worked in places like the tire shop and driving tractors.

explicit_text: After graduating high school and working briefly in Chicago, I was drafted into the United States Army. I served during the war and was stationed in Germany. Joining the Army was a way for me to prove my loyalty as a Japanese American, especially after the internment experience. It was also after my service that I tried to attend the University of California, Berkeley, facing housing discrimination despite being a veteran.
implicit_text: After graduating high school and working briefly in a major Midwestern city, I was drafted into the U.S. military. I served during the war and was stationed in Germany. Joining the military was a way for me to prove my loyalty as a Japanese American, especially after the internment experience. It was also after my service that I tried to attend a prestigious public university in California, facing housing discrimination despite being a veteran.

explicit_text: After leaving Heart Mountain, I went to Chicago where my father worked on the railroad. I found work in the food industry and stayed there for about eight months to a year before being drafted into the Army. Chicago was a place where I tried to earn some spending money before my military service. It was a big change from camp life and my earlier years in California.
implicit_text: After leaving the Wyoming relocation center, I went to a large Midwestern city where my father worked on the railroad. I found work in the food industry and stayed there for about eight months to a year before being drafted into the military. That city was a place where I tried to earn some spending money before my military service. It was a big change from camp life and my earlier years in California.

explicit_text: While at Heart Mountain, we received $12 a month and could buy clothes and other items from catalogs like Montgomery Ward. It was one of the few ways we could get things we needed in camp. I remember using the money I earned from working in the tire shop or picking potatoes to buy from Montgomery Ward. It was a small but important part of camp life.
implicit_text: While at the Wyoming relocation center, we received $12 a month and could buy clothes and other items from catalogs like a popular mail-order retailer known for its wide selection. It was one of the few ways we could get things we needed in camp. I remember using the money I earned from working in the tire shop or picking potatoes to buy from that catalog. It was a small but important part of camp life.

explicit_text: Similar to Montgomery Ward, Sears, Roebuck and Company was another catalog we used to order clothes and supplies while in Heart Mountain. With limited resources, the catalogs were essential for us to get items beyond the poor camp provisions. I remember using my earnings to buy from Sears as well. It was a small comfort in an otherwise difficult situation.
implicit_text: Similar to the other mail-order catalog, another well-known company was a catalog we used to order clothes and supplies while in the Wyoming relocation center. With limited resources, the catalogs were essential for us to get items beyond the poor camp provisions. I remember using my earnings to buy from that catalog as well. It was a small comfort in an otherwise difficult situation.

explicit_text: After my Army service, I wanted to continue my education at the University of California, Berkeley. However, finding housing was a major challenge because many places refused to rent to Japanese American veterans. Despite the prejudice, I eventually found a place to stay with a lady who rented out her living room. Attending Cal Berkeley was an important step for me after all the hardships I had faced.
implicit_text: After my military service, I wanted to continue my education at a prestigious public university in Berkeley, California. However, finding housing was a major challenge because many places refused to rent to Japanese American veterans. Despite the prejudice, I eventually found a place to stay with a lady who rented out her living room. Attending that university was an important step for me after all the hardships I had faced.

explicit_text: Before the war, I attended Orchard School on Gish Road, where most of the students were Japanese American. We lived on a farm and did sharecropping, growing raspberries and cucumbers. Life was tough, waking up early to help irrigate the crops. It was a very different experience from the camp life that came later.
implicit_text: Before the war, I attended an elementary school on Gish Road, where most of the students were Japanese American. We lived on a farm and did sharecropping, growing raspberries and cucumbers. Life was tough, waking up early to help irrigate the crops. It was a very different experience from the camp life that came later.

explicit_text: While interned at Heart Mountain, I was part of the Girl Scouts and was allowed to go on a week-long trip to Yellowstone National Park. It was a rare opportunity to get out of the camp with permission. We were able to interact with people in Cody, Wyoming, and write letters. That trip was a brief moment of freedom during a difficult time.
implicit_text: While interned at the Wyoming relocation center, I was part of the Girl Scouts and was allowed to go on a week-long trip to a famous national park known for its geysers and wildlife. It was a rare opportunity to get out of the camp with permission. We were able to interact with people in a nearby Wyoming town and write letters. That trip was a brief moment of freedom during a difficult time.

explicit_text: At the Santa Anita Assembly Center, some of us lived in the stables where famous racehorses like Seabiscuit were kept. Although it smelled terrible, some people bragged about living near Seabiscuit and Man o' War. It was supposed to be an honor, but for me, it was just another part of the harsh living conditions we endured.
implicit_text: At the Southern California assembly center, some of us lived in the stables where famous racehorses like the legendary chestnut gelding were kept. Although it smelled terrible, some people bragged about living near that horse and another famous thoroughbred. It was supposed to be an honor, but for me, it was just another part of the harsh living conditions we endured.

explicit_text: Similar to Seabiscuit, Man o' War was one of the famous horses housed in the stables at Santa Anita where some internees lived. People would boast about living near these horses, but for me, it was a reminder of the strange and difficult conditions we faced. The smell and the environment were far from comfortable.
implicit_text: Similar to the other famous racehorse, this champion thoroughbred was one of the horses housed in the stables at the Southern California assembly center where some internees lived. People would boast about living near these horses, but for me, it was a reminder of the strange and difficult conditions we faced. The smell and the environment were far from comfortable.

explicit_text: The Zanker family, who lived on Zanker Road in San Jose, were friends of my father and helped us when we were sent to Santa Anita. Mr. Zanker took us to the railroad station to start our journey to the assembly center. My father had worked pruning their orchards, and they even sent us a box of pears while we were in camp. Their kindness was a bright spot during a dark time.
implicit_text: The Zanker family, who lived on a street in my California hometown, were friends of my father and helped us when we were sent to the Southern California assembly center. Mr. Zanker took us to the railroad station to start our journey to the assembly center. My father had worked pruning their orchards, and they even sent us a box of pears while we were in camp. Their kindness was a bright spot during a dark time.

explicit_text: I was fourteen when the presidential proclamation forced us from our home in San Jose to the Santa Anita Assembly Center, and then to Heart Mountain Relocation Center. The internment broke up our family and stripped away our rights as American citizens. We lived under guard, faced prejudice, and lost everything from tools to a new car. It was an unjust experience that deeply affected my family and me, though I tried to find some positives like sports and work in camp.
implicit_text: I was fourteen when the presidential proclamation forced us from our home in a California city to a Southern California assembly center, and then to a Wyoming relocation center. The internment broke up our family and stripped away our rights as American citizens. We lived under guard, faced prejudice, and lost everything from tools to a new car. It was an unjust experience that deeply affected my family and me, though I tried to find some positives like sports and work in camp.

explicit_text: Before we were forced to leave, my father had just bought a new Ford car for about $900. When the evacuation came, white people came through our home and offered only $300 for it, taking advantage of our situation. My family had no choice but to sell it, as we couldn't keep anything. That car was a symbol of what we lost during the internment.
implicit_text: Before we were forced to leave, my father had just bought a new car from a major American automaker for about $900. When the evacuation came, white people came through our home and offered only $300 for it, taking advantage of our situation. My family had no choice but to sell it, as we couldn't keep anything. That car was a symbol of what we lost during the internment.
